text: You're about to prepare this procedure using the Incision Academy accredited by the Royal College of Surgeons. For your orientation the positioning of the body is shown. Here you can see important landmarks which will guide the subsequent incision. Our procedures are structured with a clear step-by-step approach. Here you will see the steps of the procedure.
The surgeon observes a hydropic gallbladder and pus within the peritoneal space, indicating inflammation and infection.
The surgeon inserts a suprapubic trocar under camera guidance, followed by the placement of a third trocar in the left lower abdominal space.
The surgeon inspects the peritoneal space, visually identifying the appendix typically located in the right lower quadrant, along with the terminal ileum and uterus visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic instruments to dissect adhesions attached to the abdominal wall, carefully separating the fibrous tissue from the underlying structures.
The surgeon uses a clamp to grasp the base of the appendix, revealing the infected appendix and mesoappendix. The visual inspection confirms the diagnosis of perforated appendicitis.
The surgeon ligates the base of the appendix with a laparoscopic loop. If inflammation prevents secure looping, a stapler is used distal to the base in healthy tissue to perform the appendectomy.

The surgical view displays the looped base of the appendix, the appendix itself, and the surrounding cecal pool. The surgeon cuts the appendix between the clamp and the loop, separating it from the base.

To get a quicker and more thorough preparation for the OR, click on the link below. There you will find an anatomy section relevant to this chapter, as well as a test to see if you're ready. Good luck with the preparation.
